sections.heading: Historical Information
sections.content:
Liu Bei was a warlord and the founding emperor of Shu Han during the Three Kingdoms era of China. Having risen up from the lesser noble class, he was initially a small player in the massive civil war leading up to the collapse of the Eastern Han Dynasty.

"I will definitely ride in this feather-covered (emperor's) carriage."
―Liu Bei as a child.

―Liu Bei as a child.

―Liu Bei as a child.

sections.heading: Early life
sections.content:
Liu Bei was from a distant branch of the Han Dynasty royal family. His ancestry can be traced back to Emperor Jing of Han (188-141 BC). At least up to his grandfather Liu Xiong and father Liu Hong, they likely retained aristocratic status: Liu Xiong was a xiaolian and a county magistrate, while Liu Hong is recorded as a state official of unknown rank. Liu Hong died when Liu Bei was young, and Liu Bei and his mother, whose surname is unknown, made a living by weaving straw sandals.

Liu Bei's impoverished life changed when he was 15. His uncle, Liu Yuanqi, sponsored Liu Bei and his son to study Confucian classics under Lu Zhi. Among Lu Zhi's students, Liu Bei was particularly friendly with Gongsun Zan.

Subsequently, local horse merchants Zhang Shiping and Su Shuang became friends with Liu Bei and gave him even more money. Many young people and local thugs gathered around Liu Bei, becoming his earliest henchmen.

sections.heading: Before the crisis
sections.content:
Liu Bei, serving under Zou Jing, participated in suppressing the Yellow Turban Rebellion and was appointed county police captain. Fu Zi's records state that Liu Bei participated in suppressing Zhang Chun's rebellion. After his defeat, Liu Bei feigned death to escape, but subsequently gained merit and was promoted.

Later, Emperor Ling began dismissing officials who had received their positions based on military achievements. A county inspector came to the county, and Liu Bei, believing he would be dismissed, sought a private conversation with the official. Refused, Liu Bei kidnapped and tortured the official before abandoning his post and fleeing.

Liu Bei served under Guanqiu Yi, a subordinate of He Jin, and defeated the rebels at Xiapi, being appointed assistant county magistrate. Dissatisfied with this position, Liu Bei did not take office.

The Records of Heroes[Yingxiong Ji] claims that Liu Bei was in the capital at the time, traveling with Cao Cao to Pei State to recruit soldiers and participate in the campaign against Dong Zhuo. However, this account is difficult to corroborate with other records and its authenticity is questioned.

sections.heading: Chancellor of Pingyuan
sections.content:
Subsequently, Liu Bei was appointed (or claimed to be) as the police captain of Gaotang County for unknown reasons, and later promoted to magistrate. Judging from his official position, if Liu Bei held a legitimate post, he was likely a subordinate of Jiao He[焦和], the governor of Qing Province. Jiao He originally intended to participate in the campaign against Dong Zhuo, but the Yellow Turbans in Qing Province rose up while he was away from his troops and captured many castles.

Liu Bei was defeated by Yellow Turbans and sought refuge with his friend Gongsun Zan. Gongsun Zan then sent Liu Bei to serve under his general Tian Kai in the war against Yuan Shao. Their battles were reportedly extremely bloody, with each side sending soldiers to plunder civilians in the other's territory, supposedly leaving the land barren at the time.

Liu Bei was appointed magistrate of Pingyuan County by Gongsun Zan, and later promoted to chancellor of Pingyuan State. At this time, Liu Bei is described as extremely popular; his enemy Liu Ping sent an assassin to kill him, but the assassin couldn't bring himself to do it and left after revealing the truth to Liu Bei. The Book of Wei[Weishu] states that Liu Bei was charitable and generous, sitting with the lowest-ranking nobles and eating from the same pot, without excluding them because of their status. Unaware of the truth, Liu Bei's courteous treatment of the assassin caused him to abandon his mission.

sections.heading: Governor of Xu Province
sections.content:
Even though Liu Bei and Gongsun Zan supposedly had a brotherly relationship, Gongsun Zan didn't seem to value him. Liu Bei's army at this time consisted of a thousand private soldiers, Wuhuan cavalry, and starving people he had forcibly conscripted. When Cao Cao attacked Tao Qian, Gongsun Zan sent Tian Kai to aid him, with Liu Bei also serving under him. Liu Bei and Cao Bao, a general under Tao Qian, attempted a pincer attack on Cao Cao but were defeated. However, Liu Bei clearly gained Tao Qian's favor, who provided him with 4,000 regular soldiers, leading Liu Bei to pledge allegiance to Tao Qian.

The local wealthy man Mi Zhu claimed that Tao Qian's will stipulated that Liu Bei should take over Xu Province. Meanwhile, Chen Deng and Kong Rong encouraged Liu Bei to seize Xu Province and not cede the land to their original ally, the supposedly morally corrupt Yuan Shu. However, another of Liu Bei's advisors, Chen Qun, believed this was dangerous and advised Liu Bei against it. Liu Bei ultimately accepted the proposal and declared himself governor. Liu Bei then sent Chen Deng to ally with Yuan Shao (at this time, Gongsun Zan and Yuan Shao were still at war). Yuan Shu attacked, and Liu Bei confronted him, while Cao Cao appointed Liu Bei as General Who Guards the East.

After being defeated by Cao Cao, Lü Bu went to seek refuge with Liu Bei. It is said that Lü Bu's behavior angered Liu Bei, but Liu Bei did not retaliate and sent Lü Bu to defend Pei County (Xiao Pei) to the west. Liu Bei ordered his general Zhang Fei to defend Xiapi. Zhang Fei killed Cao Bao, a remnant of Tao Qian's forces, and Cao Bao's men summoned Lü Bu. Lü Bu defeated Zhang Fei and seized Xiapi and Liu Bei's family. When Liu Bei arrived at Xiapi, his army had already scattered. Liu Bei rallied his defeated troops and went to attack Guangling, then fought Yuan Shu, but was defeated. Liu Bei's supplies ran out, and his men resorted to cannibalism to survive.

"My governor, I dare not listen to you unless you take office."
―Chen Deng persuaded Liu Bei to take Xu Province himself.

―Chen Deng persuaded Liu Bei to take Xu Province himself.

―Chen Deng persuaded Liu Bei to take Xu Province himself.

sections.heading: Governor of Yu Province
sections.content:
Liu Bei surrendered to Lü Bu. Lü Bu welcomed him with great ceremony, returned Liu Bei's family, and invited him to join forces against Yuan Shu. Fearful, Liu Bei requested permission to garrison Xiaopei. Yuan Shu sent Ji Ling to completely eliminate Liu Bei, but Lü Bu unexpectedly forced them to negotiate peace. Yang Feng and Han Xian attempted to unite with Liu Bei against Lü Bu, but Liu Bei assassinated Yang Feng at a banquet. Han Xian escaped but was killed by others. After mobilizing an army of tens of thousands at Xiaopei, Liu Bei was defeated by Lü Bu personally, and Liu Bei surrendered to Cao Cao.

Cheng Yu considered Liu Bei a dangerous figure and advised Cao Cao to execute him immediately. Guo Jia opposed this decision, arguing that Cao Cao valued talent and should not leave a bad reputation for killing virtuous men, but also warned Cao Cao to be vigilant. Cao Cao seemed to have no suspicion of Liu Bei, appointing him Governor of Yu Province, providing him with soldiers and supplies, and sending him to fight against Lü Bu.

Lü Bu sent a caravan to Zhang Yang to buy horses. Liu Bei plundered Lü Bu's caravan. Lü Bu dispatched Gao Shun and Zhang Liao to attack Liu Bei. Liu Bei suffered a major defeat, Pei City fell, and Liu Bei fled alone, his family captured by Lü Bu. Cao Cao's reinforcements, led by Xiahou Dun, were also defeated. Cao Cao personally led the attack, and the fleeing Liu Bei joined Cao Cao's army in Liang State. Ultimately, Lü Bu was defeated, Xiapi was captured, and Liu Bei's family was released. Some records state that Lü Bu asked Liu Bei to plead with Cao Cao on his behalf, but Liu Bei, taking advantage of the situation, persuaded Cao Cao to execute Lü Bu.

sections.heading: Governor of Yu Province(II)
sections.content:
Cao Cao promoted Liu Bei to Left General, treating him with the utmost respect, sharing the same carriage and seat. However, Liu Bei was now involved in Dong Cheng's plot to assassinate Cao Cao. Dong Cheng claimed to have obtained a secret imperial edict hidden in the emperor's belt. Liu Bei initially had no intention of participating, but when Cao Cao dined with Liu Bei, he said, "The only heroes in the world are you, my governor, and me. Benchu and the others are insignificant." Fearing Cao Cao's ulterior motives, Liu Bei conspired with Dong Cheng.

Yuan Shu secretly reconciled with Yuan Shao, intending to offer the imperial seal and throne to him. Cao Cao dispatched Liu Bei, Zhu Ling, and Lu Zhao to intercept and drive Yuan Shu away. Cheng Yu and Guo Jia persuaded Cao Cao to recall Liu Bei, but Cao Cao's messenger failed to arrive in time. Liu Bei went to Xu Province, murdered the newly appointed governor Che Zhou, and joined forces with Chang Xi, amassing tens of thousands of troops.

Meanwhile, Yuan Shao's army had begun its southward advance. Cao Cao sent his subordinates Liu Dai and Wang Zhong to attack Liu Bei, but they were defeated. Liu Bei told Liu Dai and the others, "Even a hundred of you couldn't defeat me. If Cao Cao himself came, the consequences would be unpredictable." Dong Cheng and his cronies' conspiracy was discovered, and they were all executed. Cao Cao decided to personally attack Liu Bei. Liu Bei's scouts reported seeing Cao Cao's banners, which Liu Bei personally verified before fleeing north to Yuan Tan with a small group. Cao Cao captured Xiapi, taking Liu Bei's family prisoner, and Guan Yu surrendered.

Because Liu Bei had previously used his prerogative as governor to select Yuan Tan as a talented scholar, Yuan Shao and his son were especially welcoming to Liu Bei, traveling a great distance outside the city to greet him. Liu Bei, as a cavalry general, attacked Yanjin with Wen Chou, but they fell into Cao Cao's trap and suffered a major defeat; Wen Chou was killed. Upon learning that Liu Bei was alive, Guan Yu defected to Yuan Shao's army. Yuan Shao sent Liu Bei to plunder capital Xu with Liu Pi, a Yellow Turban leader from Runan, but they were defeated by Cao Cao's general Cao Ren. Liu Bei persuaded Yuan Shao to send him to ally with Liu Biao. Yuan Shao again sent Liu Bei to Runan to join forces with another Yellow Turban leader, Gong Du. Liu Bei and Gong Du together killed Cai Yang, sent by Cao Cao. Yuan Shao was defeated in the Battle of Guandu. Cao Cao personally attacked Liu Bei, who fled to Liu Biao, and Gong Du and the others also fled.

sections.heading: Governor of Yu Province(III)
sections.content:
Liu Biao treated Liu Bei with the utmost respect, reinforced his troops, and stationed him at Xinye to defend the north. Many powerful families in Jing Privince defected to Liu Bei, but Liu Biao, suspicious of Liu Bei's intentions, secretly took precautions. During Cao Cao's conquest of Hebei, Liu Biao sent Liu Bei to attack Ye County, where Cao Cao's generals Xiahou Dun and Yu Jin came to defend. Liu Bei burned his own camp and feigned a retreat. Xiahou Dun and his men pursued him to Bowang, where they were ambushed and defeated by Liu Bei's forces. Li Dian rescued Xiahou Dun and the others. When Cao Cao campaigned against the Wuhuan, Liu Bei persuaded Liu Biao to attack, but Liu Biao refused. Liu Biao was said to favor his younger son Liu Cong and distance himself from his eldest son Liu Qi, and Liu Bei was said to be in cahoots with Liu Qi.

Cao Cao launched his southern campaign, and Liu Biao died of illness. The Wei Shu states that Liu Biao intended to cede Jing Privince to Liu Bei, but Pei Songzhi believed this to be fabricated. Liu Cong succeeded Liu Biao and submitted a letter of surrender to Cao Cao. Liu Bei was stationed in Fancheng at the time, only realizing Cao Cao's army had reached Wan when he was discovered, and thus retreated to Xiangyang. Zhuge Liang instigated Liu Bei to attack Liu Cong and seize Jing Privince, but Liu Bei claimed he "couldn't bear to." Liu Bei called out to Liu Cong from below the city walls, but Liu Cong reportedly dared not respond, leading many of Liu Cong's officials and the people of Jing Privince to decide to follow Liu Bei. Liu Bei also went to Liu Biao's tomb to weep and pay his respects. When he advanced to Dangyang, Liu Bei had 100,000 men and thousands of wagons. Liu Bei sent Guan Yu to secure Jiangling. Liu Bei's confidants believed that although they had a large number of men, they lacked armored troops, a dangerous situation, and that Liu Bei should immediately go to Jiangling himself to secure the weapons there. However, Liu Bei said, "The foundation for great undertakings is people; now that they have surrendered to me, how can I abandon them?"

After accepting Liu Cong's surrender, Cao Cao learned of the military supplies in Jiangling and, fearing Liu Bei would seize them, hastily marched to Xiangyang. Upon learning that Liu Bei was heading south, Cao Cao dispatched Cao Chun with 5,000 elite cavalry in pursuit. Cao Chun caught up with Liu Bei at Changban of Dangyang. Liu Bei abandoned his 100,000 followers and his own family, fleeing with only Zhuge Liang, Zhang Fei, Zhao Yun, and a few dozen close confidants. These people and supplies were captured by Cao Cao's army. Two of Liu Bei's daughters were captured, while Zhao Yun managed to secure the safe passage of Liu Bei's concubine, Lady Gan, and son, Liu Shan. Some who had previously allied with Liu Bei, such as Xu Shu, switched allegiance to Cao Cao after their families were captured.

Liu Bei fled to the riverbank, where he happened to meet with Guan Yu's marines. He then sailed away and joined forces with Liu Qi in Jiangxia, bringing over 10,000 men to Xiakou. Liu Bei sent Zhuge Liang to seek an alliance with Sun Quan. They negotiated terms with Lu Su, met with Sun Quan, and swore an alliance. Sun Quan dispatched Zhou Yu and Cheng Pu with marines to resist Cao Cao. According to the Jiangbiao Zhuan, Liu Bei sent his subordinates to negotiate with Zhou Yu in a high-handed manner. Zhou Yu refused to "abandon his post", forcing Liu Bei to meet him in person, and refused to allow Liu Bei to summon Lu Su for the same reason. Unsure if Zhou Yu and his allies had defeated Cao Cao, Liu Bei sent Guan Yu and Zhang Fei with 2,000 men to observe from behind Zhou Yu's lines. The allied forces defeated Cao Cao at Chibi, burned his warships, and pursued him to Nanjun. Subsequently, a plague broke out, and Cao Cao's army retreated.

sections.heading: Governor of Jing Province
sections.content:
Initially, Zhou Yu himself served as the prefect of Nanjun, leaving Liu Bei to garrison at Youjiangkou. Liu Bei renamed Youjiangkou to Gong'an. Liu Bei then launched a southern campaign against Jing Province, and according to the Records of the Three Kingdoms, four prefectures surrendered. According to the Sanfu Juelu, Jin Xuan was killed by Liu Bei's army. The Records of the Three Kingdoms suggests that Liu Bei immediately took control of these territories, but based on the ranks of Wu generals, it is possible that Liu Bei initially ceded these lands to Sun Quan.

Liu Qi died, and his subordinates chose Liu Bei as the new governor. Seeing Liu Bei's power expanding again, Sun Quan arranged for Liu Bei to marry his sister to solidify the alliance. According to the Records of the Duke of Shanyang, Liu Bei was dissatisfied with the meeting, believing that "Sun the Chariots and Cavalry's upper body is long and his lower body is short, making it difficult for him to be of lower status than others. I cannot meet with him again." Previously, Liu Bei's rank of General of the Left was higher than Sun Quan's miscellaneous general rank (a "legitimate" rank obtained from the Han court before their confrontation with Cao Cao). When they began their fight against Cao Cao, Liu Bei "recommended" Sun Quan to be General of Chariots and Cavalry—a position far higher than Liu Bei's—as a gesture of submission, which Liu Bei disliked.

Some remnants of Liu Biao's faction defected to Liu Bei. Liu Bei felt the territory allocated to him by Zhou Yu was insufficient to support his population and subsequently "borrowed" land from Jing Province from Sun Quan.

Sun Quan sent an envoy to invite Liu Bei to join him in attacking Liu Zhang in Yi Province. Liu Bei's advisor, Yin Guan, suggested that they feign instability in their territory and seize Yi Province themselves. Liu Bei followed Yin Guan's advice. Sun Quan sent Sun Yu to cross Jing Province to attack Liu Zhang, but Liu Bei refused them passage, saying, "If you seize Shu, I will let my hair down (become a barbarian) to avoid breaking my promise."

sections.heading: Governor of Yi Province
sections.content:
In 211, Liu Zhang learned that Cao Cao had begun his campaign against Zhang Lu and feared he would soon be attacked as well. Zhang Song, one of Liu Zhang's assistant governors, advised him to welcome Liu Bei, Cao Cao's nemesis. In fact, Zhang Song and his associates Fa Zheng and Meng Da conspired to sell Yi Province to Liu Bei. Liu Zhang adopted their suggestion and personally welcomed Liu Bei. Zhang Song, Fa Zheng, and Liu Bei's strategist Pang Tong advised Liu Bei to assassinate Liu Zhang directly, but Liu Bei considered it too dangerous and did not heed their advice. Liu Zhang, completely unaware of this, bribed Liu Bei with hundreds of millions of coins, a large number of soldiers, and weapons, entrusting Liu Bei with command of Liu Zhang's army at Baishui Pass to conquer Zhang Lu. Instead of confronting Zhang Lu, Liu Bei bribed Liu Zhang's subordinates.

In 212, Cao Cao attacked Sun Quan at Ruxukou, and Sun Quan sought help from Liu Bei. Liu Bei did not send aid, but instead declared to Liu Zhang that Guan Yu would soon be defeated by Yue Jin and that Zhang Lu was an insignificant opponent, demanding 10,000 soldiers and more supplies from Liu Zhang. Liu Zhang only provided 4,000 men and demanded half that amount of supplies. Liu Bei then tricked his subordinates, claiming that Liu Zhang intended to force them to fight without paying their any money. Zhang Song, unaware of this ruse, mistakenly believed Liu Bei was truly leaving and wrote to him: "Now that the great undertaking is about to be accomplished, how can you abandon this opportunity and leave!" Zhang Song's brother, Zhang Su, learned of this and reported Zhang Song and Liu Bei's betrayal to Liu Zhang, who then arrested and executed Zhang Song. Liu Zhang wrote to his generals, demanding that they no longer accept bribes from Liu Bei. Yang Huai and Gao Pei, the garrison commanders of Baishui Pass, had previously been close to Liu Bei. Liu Bei lured them there, fabricated charges against them, and had them killed. He then kidnapped the families of the Baishui Pass soldiers, forcing them to fight for him. Liu Zhang sent Liu Gui, Ling Bao, Zhang Ren, and Deng Xian to resist, but they were all defeated. Liu Zhang sent Li Yan to resist Liu Bei at Mianzhu Pass, but Li Yan surrendered.

In 214, after a year-long siege, Liu Bei captured Luo City, which was held by Liu Xun, the son of Liu Zhang. He then besieged Chengdu for several dozen days, after which Liu Zhang surrendered. Liu Bei declared himself Governor of Yi Province. He opened all the warehouses in Chengdu, allowing his soldiers to freely take what they wanted. He also ordered his soldiers to plunder the property of the city's civilians, returning the grain and cloth. Because Liu Bei had distributed too many rewards, he followed Liu Ba's advice and imitated Dong Zhuo's scheme, minting new coins and declaring them worth 100 coins to trade with the civilians. Within months, he had regained a large amount of supplies. It is said that Liu Bei reformed Liu Zhang's corrupt policies and appointed various talented people, even those who had previously served Liu Zhang.

"I, Liu Bei, and Liu Zhang are both members of the imperial clan, hoping to rely on the might of heroes to restore the Han court. Now that Liu Zhang has offended those around you, I alone am filled with fear and dare not speak of such matters. I beg for leniency. If my request is not granted, I will return to the mountains with my hair down."
―Liu Bei tricked Sun Quan into abandoning his expedition.

―Liu Bei tricked Sun Quan into abandoning his expedition.

―Liu Bei tricked Sun Quan into abandoning his expedition.

sections.heading: King of Hanzhong
sections.content:
In 215, upon learning that Liu Bei had seized Yi Province for him, Sun Quan sent an envoy demanding the return of Jing Province. Liu Bei declared, "I will return Jing Province after I conquer Liang Province." This greatly angered Sun Quan, who dispatched Lü Meng to attack southern Jing Province, capturing Changsha, Lingling, and Guiyang. Liu Bei mobilized 50,000 troops to fight Sun Quan, but upon learning that Cao Cao had begun attacking Zhang Lu, he negotiated peace with Sun Quan. Cao Cao defeated Zhang Lu in Hanzhong, and Liu Bei sent Huang Quan to welcome Zhang Lu, but Zhang Lu ultimately chose to surrender to Cao Cao. Cao Cao left Xiahou Yuan and Zhang He to guard Hanzhong.

In 218, Liu Bei personally attacked Hanzhong. In 219, Liu Bei's general Huang Zhong killed Xiahou Yuan at Dingjun Mountain. Cao Cao personally attacked, but Liu Bei defended the mountain, avoiding direct combat. Cao Cao forcibly relocated nearby civilians before retreating, and Liu Bei seized Hanzhong, proclaiming himself King of Hanzhong. Liu Bei's ministers began preparing political propaganda for his imperial ambitions: Li Yan claimed a yellow dragon had appeared, and Guan Yu claimed they had accidentally obtained a jade seal bestowed by the gods.

Liu Bei sent Meng Da to attack Fangling and Shangyong, but then sent his adopted son Liu Feng to manage Meng Da. Meanwhile, Guan Yu, taking advantage of a flood in Jing Province, defeated Yu Jin, besieged Fancheng, and recruited a large number of bandits to operate near capital Xu, reportedly enough to "shake the whole of China". However, Sun Quan decided to join forces with Cao Cao to seize Jing Province. Guan Yu was subsequently defeated by Xu Huang of Wei, and Jing Province was also captured by Lü Meng of Wu. Guan Yu's feigned surrender failed, and he was killed by Sun Quan's army.

"...Previously, when Guan Yu besieged Fan and Xiangyang, Zhang Jia and Wang Xiu, men from Xiangyang, presented Your Highness with a jade seal. The seal was hidden in the Han River and concealed in a deep spring. Its brilliance shone brightly, and its spiritual light reached the heavens."
―Shu ministers recall alleged miracles during the Battle of Fan Castle

―Shu ministers recall alleged miracles during the Battle of Fan Castle

―Shu ministers recall alleged miracles during the Battle of Fan Castle

sections.heading: Emperor Zhaolie
sections.content:
In 220, Cao Cao died. Liu Bei sent envoys to attempt reconciliation with Cao Pi. One account states that Cao Pi, disgusted by this, executed the envoys; another states that Cao Pi, by this time already emperor, replied to Liu Bei with an official document. In 221, Liu Bei falsely claimed that Emperor Xian had been killed, and with the support of his own officials, declared himself the new emperor of the Han Dynasty (later known as Shu Han).

Liu Feng and Meng Da were at odds. Liu Feng seized Meng Da's entourage, prompting Meng Da to defect to Cao Pi. Cao Pi sent Meng Da, along with Xu Huang and Xiahou Shang, to attack Liu Feng. Meng Da wrote a letter urging Liu Feng to surrender, claiming that Liu Bei intended for his own son, Liu Shan, to inherit the throne, and that Liu Feng's position was in danger. Liu Feng refused, fought Meng Da, was defeated, and fled back to Chengdu. Liu Bei condemned Liu Feng for not assisting Guan Yu in the siege of Fancheng and for mistreating Meng Da. Zhuge Liang seized the opportunity to instigate Liu Bei to execute Liu Feng. Liu Feng said, "I regret not heeding Meng Zidu's advice." Liu Bei wept over this.

After Liu Bei declared himself emperor, he prepared to wage war against Wu to seize Jing Province. Sun Quan sent an envoy to request peace, but Liu Bei refused. Liu Bei then sent Ma Liang to bribe the local Wuxi barbarians to join him. Liu Bei was blocked on the Yiling Road by Wu generals led by Lu Xun. Liu Bei was forced to establish a camp in a narrow terrain. Liu Bei tried to lure Lu Xun into battle, but Lu Xun did not fall for the trap.

After a year of stalemate (222), Lu Xun used fire to break through Liu Bei's camp. Liu Bei retreated to nearby Saddle Mountain, where Lu Xun commanded an attack on the mountain, resulting in a major defeat for Liu Bei. Liu Bei's warships and weapons were almost entirely lost. Liu Bei abandoned his troops and fled along the mountain path, pursued by Wu general Sun Huan. Liu Bei managed to escape the pursuit and entered Baidi City. Wu generals such as Pan Zhang requested to continue the attack, but Lu Xun stopped them, saying that it was more important to pay attention to Cao Pi's movements.

Liu Bei and Sun Quan exchanged envoys to reconcile. Both sides' biographies claim the other yielded. The Biography of Former Lord in the Records of the Three Kingdoms states that Sun Quan, learning Liu Bei was stationed in Baidi City, was greatly fearful and proactively sent envoys to sue for peace. The Jiangbiao Zhuan and Wu Shu state that Sun Quan told his officials that Liu Bei had written a letter apologizing to him, which he was willing to accept, and sent Zheng Quan as an envoy to negotiate peace. Liu Bei questioned Zheng Quan why Sun Quan had not replied acknowledging his imperial status, to which Zheng Quan replied that Sun Quan did not want to respond to the accusations of a usurper, and Liu Bei felt ashamed.

sections.heading: Death
sections.content:
In 223, Liu Bei died after a serious illness. He appointed Zhuge Liang and Li Yan as regents. He declared to Zhuge Liang that if he was dissatisfied with Liu Shan, he could replace the emperor himself. He told the successor Liu Shan that he should respect Zhuge Liang as he would his father and strive to be a person of excellent virtue.

After Liu Bei's death, Sun Quan appointed Shi Xie to continue instigating uprisings by the Nanman to overthrow Shu. Zhuge Liang suppressed these rebellions. Convinced that Wei would be inherited by the undervalued Cao Rui, Zhuge Liang formally allied with Wu and began his famous Northern Expeditions. These achievements were embellished in later literary works, but due to the disparity in national strength, they yielded almost no real military gains. Zhuge Liang's excessive work, even interfering in the affairs of many low-ranking officials, ultimately led to his death from overwork.

Chen Shou did not focus on the political situation after Zhuge Liang's death, but some general descriptions claim that the rule gradually deteriorated. Liu Shan was unable to control the power struggles among Huang Hao, Yan Yu, Chen Zhi, Jiang Wei, and others, and appointed Zhuge Liang's son, Zhuge Zhan, who was reportedly very incompetent. In 263, Liu Shan, who had ruled for 40 years, surrendered to Wei.

Liu Bei's decision to appoint Zhuge Liang as regent was seen at the time as a remarkable act of mutual trust between the ruler and his minister, and it has been praised as a moral exemplar in later generations.

"...When Sir She[She Yuan] arrived, he said the Prime Minister praised your wisdom and capacity, saying your abilities have greatly improved, exceeding his expectations. If you can truly achieve this, what more could I worry about! Keep up the good work! Do not do evil because it is insignificant, nor neglect good because it is insignificant. Only virtue and wisdom can win people's hearts. Your father's virtue was lacking; do not emulate him."
―Liu Bei's will to Liu Shan.

―Liu Bei's will to Liu Shan.

―Liu Bei's will to Liu Shan.

sections.heading: Personal Info
sections.content:
Liu Bei was approximately 174cm tall. Chen Shou claimed that Liu Bei had extremely long arms and enormous ears: his hands could hang down past his knees when standing upright, and his ears were visible from his eyes. This record is likely an exaggeration; ancient Chinese political propaganda often fabricated unusual physical features to claim that someone had received the Mandate of Heaven. Considering that he was described as having "large ears" in other records, his ears may indeed have been larger than the average person's.

Even as a student of the great Confucian scholar Lu Zhi, Liu Bei did not enjoy studying Confucianism, but instead preferred luxuries such as dogs, horses, music, and fine clothes.

Liu Bei was taciturn, reserved in his emotions, and humble in his treatment of others.

Liu Bei had several wives and sons, but they were all lost and their whereabouts unknown when he was defeated. Liu Bei initially designated Kou Feng (also known as Liu Feng), who had maternal lineage from the Han Dynasty, as his heir. However, after Liu Bei had his own son, Liu Shan, Liu Feng gradually fell out of favor. Zhuge Liang instigated Liu Bei to fabricate charges and execute Liu Feng. This is one of the few instances where Zhuge Liang, portrayed as a saintly figure, is directly depicted negatively. However, this action prevented the Shu Kingdom from experiencing the devastating succession struggles seen in Wei or Wu. This action was similar to that of Hashiba Hideyoshi, but the consequences were the opposite.

The novel gives the impression that Liu Bei's royal lineage was extremely noble and rare. Despite his claims, his relation to the Imperial bloodline was noted to be quite distant. It's highly likely that Liu Bei didn't ascertain his generational relationship with the current emperor, as he didn't attempt to recognize a deceased emperor as his adoptive father, as Liu Xiu, the founder of the Eastern Han Dynasty, did. This is understandable in some ways: Liu Ai, the official in charge of royal genealogy, served in the court established by Cao Cao and was likely a loyalist to him.

sections.heading: Other notes
sections.content:
Before establishing his own empire, Liu Bei served several rulers: Gongsun Zan, Tao Qian, Lü Bu, Cao Cao, Yuan Shao, Liu Biao, and Sun Quan, and was also employed by Liu Zhang for a time. At times, Liu Bei switched allegiance between two clearly hostile forces. For this, Chen Shou declared that Liu Bei was "incapable of submitting to others." Some historical records about Liu Bei describe him as cunning and prone to betrayal. However, this should be taken into account that the records of Wei and Wu may be biased, and that the forces destroyed by these two states do not leave many records (for example, Yuan Shao and Yuan Shu clearly assisted Cao Cao and Sun Ce's cause).

The novel stereotypes Liu Bei as someone who, aside from his supposedly high moral character, is utterly incompetent and relies on his superhuman warriors. Regardless, he was often cited for his charisma and determination, which was often enough to get him back on his feet despite the constant setbacks in a majority of his other campaigns: he served under Gongsun Zan for a long time against Yuan Shao.  He also won a few notable battles such as against Cao Cao's subordinates on multiple occasions, and chasing him after Zhou Yu's fire attack done. Specific deeds and/or schemes attributed to the novel version of Zhuge Liang (such as the fire attack at Bowangpo) were actually credited to Liu Bei himself in history.

Another common myth was that Liu Bei threw down Liu Shan for endangering Zhao Yun at Changban which in turn caused him to be mentally damaged; this event was only exclusive to the novel. Also in regards to his family, he abandoned members on three notable occasions though it was in dire circumstances. Historical records mention that he had several wives, at least one son, and two daughters whom he abandoned. This led him to initially adopt Liu Feng as his heir, but Liu Feng lost his favor after Liu Shan was born.

Cao Pi had deduced that Liu Bei would lose his campaign at Yiling because he felt that thanks to Liu Bei's recruited talent that he achieved a portion of his goals in the first place. However, others have noted that Cao Pi didn't take his own advice seeing how he ended up having stalemates against Wu and not making any significant progress against them.

His relationship with both Guan Yu and Zhang Fei while not on the account of the famous Peach Garden Oath in the novel, was that of close-partnership with no special relations. Some records however, have noted them to be rather close enough to be mentioned together, implying a friendship between the three that was legitimate through thick and thin times.

sections.heading: Romance of the Three Kingdoms
sections.content: Liu Bei from Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

sections.heading: Humble Beginnings
sections.content:
Liu Bei's father Liu Hong was recommended as having filial piety and an upright behavior, and was given a minor office. However, he died early into his career, and Liu Bei treated his widowed mother with great devotion. Impoverished, the two resorted to weaving mats and selling sandals in order to make a living. They lived within a county village in Zhuo county called "Two-Story Mulberry", after the tree some forty feet southeast of the village. The mulberry tree was tall, and could be seen from afar. It spread wide, and acted like a baldachin canopy on a carraige. At one point in time, a fortune teller predicted, "An eminent man will come from this house."

Whilst playing with other boys beneath the mulberry tree, Liu Bei often said, "When I'm Emperor, my chariot will have a canopy like this." Liu Bei's uncle, Liu Yuanqi was impressed by his nephew and praised him highly many times. He usually came by to help his poverty-stricken family in their tasks. At the age of fifteen, Liu Bei was sent away to study under Lu Zhi with Gongsun Zan, to whom he began a close friendship with.

"This is no ordinary child."
―Liu Bei's uncle, regarding his nephew.

―Liu Bei's uncle, regarding his nephew.

―Liu Bei's uncle, regarding his nephew.

sections.heading: Oath of the Peach Garden
sections.content:
At the age twenty-eight, he became approximately five feet and seven inches in height, was described as having elongated ear lobes with arms that reached below his knees, and also having widely-positioned eyes. His face was similar to jade in its flawlessness, and he also had rouge lips. He stood up where a call was posted within Zhuo county, calling for any volunteers against the Yellow Turban rebellion, a massive uprising by local farmers displeased by the state of the Han empire.

Liu Bei let out a long sigh. Someone behind him asked, "Why such long sighs?", to which Liu Bei responded, "A real man should be serving his emperor in the hour of peril." He turned around to see a panther-faced man whose height neared six feet He had huge round eyes, with heavy cheeks, whiskers resembling that of a tiger's, and with a booming voice that thundered as it rang. Liu Bei asked the man's name to which he introduced himself as Zhang Fei, so Liu Bei spoke his own name. The two conversed their desire to assist the Empire against the revolt, and Zhang Fei proposed that the they work together.

The pair were having a drink at the local tavern, when a tall fellow, approximately six feet and nine inches, walked inside and sat. His beard was two feet long, and his skin was red, with thick eyebrows and a face that was reminiscent of a phoenix's.

Liu Bei invited the man over to ask who he was. The wanderer hastily replied in saying he was Guan Yu, and had been on the run from the law after killing a scandalous man in Jieliang five or six years before. He also shared interests in defeating the Yellow Turbans, and thus Zhang Fei asked if the other two would like to become sworn brothers, to which they agreed. The three went to Zhang Fei's peach garden, and after sacrificing animals for a ritual, swore themselves as brothers in the Peach Garden Oath.

The next day some blacksmiths craft a pair of ancient swords for Liu Bei. They march off to battle with the 500 others gathered, under Imperial Protector Liu Yan. Liu Yan respects Liu Bei for his ancestry and gives him command of the van. They soon come across a Yellow Turban army of around 50,000 led by Cheng Yuanzhi. Cheng Yuanzhi personally charges at Zhang Fei but is cut in half by Guan Yu in one stoke. The rebel army throw their swords away and retreat.

They next march to Qingzhou City which is under siege by the Yellow Turbans. After a brief skirmish, Liu Bei and his men are forced to retreat. They then devise a plan to ambush the Yellow Turban troops. Liu Bei's troops draw the Yellow Turbans and then retreat. The Yellow Turbans pursue and are attacked on both sides by Guan Yu and Zhang Fei's men as well as Imperial Protector Gong Jing leading a force from the city to attack behind them. The rebel army is decimated and the siege is lifted.

Next they march to Yingchuan to help Imperial Commander Lu Zhi, who is fighting against the Yellow Turban army personally led by Zhang Jiao. They arrive at the battle to late, having already been won by the Han forces. They then come across Lu Zhi being carted away in a cage. They then retrace their steps back to the city of Zhou, when they come across a Han force being attacked by Zhang Jiao's forces. The three brothers rush their force in and push the Yellow Turban's back and rescue the commanding general Dong Zhuo. They then escort him back to camp. When he finds out they are not officers, he treats them with disrespect.

Nonetheless, as a reward for his efforts, Liu Bei becomes the a county prefect, which he fulfills with the admiration of the peasants. Unfortunately, a corrupt inspector arrives to assess Liu Bei. Offering nothing to bribe the inspector, Liu Bei laments not being able to serve more. While speaking with Guan Yu later on, Liu Bei is aghast at seeing Zhang Fei tie the inspector in the middle of the county and beat him with a whip. Surrendering his seal of authority, Liu Bei admonishes the inspector before leaving. Later on the, the vengeful inspector spreads false rumors that Liu Bei encouraged his beating, forcing Liu to flee to his old classmate, Gongsun Zan.

sections.heading: Alliance against Dong Zhuo and Governor
sections.content:
Not long after his arrival, Yuan Shao issues an edict requesting governors to band together and combat Dong Zhuo, who has abused the emperor's power. Joining Gongsun Zan, Liu Bei accompanies the lords to Sishui Gate, which is guarded by Hua Xiong.

After Hua Xiong slays three commanders and defeats Sun Jian, the governors are left speechless at what to do next. Guan Yu volunteers and later slays Hua Xiong. As a reward for the deed, the oath brothers are sent gifts by Cao Cao. During the main battle at Hulao Gate, Lu Bu proves unstoppable against the allied forces, but Zhang Fei rides out to challenge the general. Once Zhang Fei begins struggling, however, Liu Bei and Guan Yu both ride out to aid their brother.

Unable to fend off all three of his opponents, Lu Bu is forced to withdraw back into the pass. Liu Bei then pushes the siege further. After Dong Zhuo flees to Chang'an and the alliance is dissolved, Liu Bei returns to Gongsun Zan's service. Liu Bei later assists Gongsun Zan at Jieqiao, meeting the young Zhao Yun in the process, who is impressed by Liu's charisma with the common folk. He later leaves to Pingyuan to assume the role of governor. His stay would be short as Tao Qian, governor of Xuzhou is attacked by Cao Cao in retaliation to Cao Song's death. Liu Bei arrives, but Cao Cao is forced to withdraw shortly after due to Lu Bu's attack on Yan Province. On his deathbed, Liu Bei is pushed by the dying Tao Qian as well as both his oath brothers and Xuzhou's officials to take over as governor. After hearted pleas, Liu Bei relents to honor Tao's wish.

Later on, Lu Bu is defeated by Cao Cao, and requests refuge in Xuzhou. Owing to Lu Bu's attack for Cao Cao's retreat, Liu Bei accepts, against the wishes of Guan and Zhang. With Yuan Shao defeating Gongsun Zan and formed an alliance with Liu Bei, while Ji Ling was sent out to attack Xuzhou.

Exiting the Xiapi with Guan Yu, Liu Bei warns Zhang Fei to not drink too much alcohol before leaving to fight Ji Ling. Zhang Fei does not listen to his brother's advice and loses much of the province as a result to Lu Bu. Lu Bu later forces both Liu Bei and Ji Ling to withdraw their troops. Unable to chase Lu Bu out, Liu Bei is assigned to guard Xiaopei, but a messenger from Cao Cao arrives, offering him haven at Xuchang instead.

sections.heading: On the run
sections.content:
Liu Bei accepts Cao Cao's offer, fleeing from Xiaopei to escape Lu Bu's wrath. Forming a joint army with Cao Cao, the pair proceed to defeat Lu Bu's forces at Xiapi. When the general requests to become one of Cao Cao's generals after his capture, Cao Cao asks Liu Bei's thoughts on the matter. In response, Liu Bei sarcastically asked Cao to take Lu as an adoptive son instead. Liu Bei's words cement Cao Cao's decision to execute Lu Bu. After Lu Bu's death, Liu Bei is kept at close watch at Xuchang.

Hoping to reduce suspicions, Liu Bei refuses to attend political matters and focuses more in attending to his vegetable garden instead. His behavior, however, still garners Cao's attention, and he is given a personal visit by Cao Cao. As the two speak, Cao Cao asks Liu to name people he believes are heroes. Liu Bei names Sun Ce, whom Cao Cao thinks is too young and inexperienced, Yuan Shao and Yuan Shu, both of whom Cao thinks can be dealt with with ease. Unable to think of anyone else, Cao Cao replies that he believes only the both of them are truly worthy of the title, startling Liu Bei into dropping his chopsticks.

Feigning the reasoning to be lightning, Liu Bei's opportunity to escape comes in the form of Yuan Shu's declaration as emperor. Given troops to defeat Yuan Shu, Liu Bei destroys Yuan Shu's forces, and the latter eventually dies of depression from the crushing loss. Instead of returning to Xuchang, however, Liu Bei simply surrenders his seal, and leads his troops to kill Che Zhou and retake the province. Not long after, Cao Cao sends a retaliatory force to Xuzhou, forcing Liu Bei to flee to Yuan Shao without Guan Yu.

When Yan Liang is killed by Guan Yu at Baima, Yuan Shao wishes to kill Liu Bei, but because only a vague description of the killer was used, it is not enough for Yuan to finish the order. Liu Bei is then sent to act as Wen Chou's support for the upcoming battle at Yanjin. Wen Chou, however, is ambushed and slain by Guan Yu, and scouts are able to give a full description of Guan to Yuan Shao. Liu Bei escapes execution once again by volunteering to have Guan switch sides. As soon as he writes the letter, however, Liu Bei secretly slips away from Yuan Shao's forces to reunite with Guan Yu.

sections.heading: Building strength
sections.content:
On the way, Liu Bei meets with Zhao Yun once again, and the latter joins Liu as a shortly after. The group take refuge at Runan, but Cao Cao, fresh from his victory against Yuan Shao, sends his men to attack Liu Bei. Forced to flee once again, Liu Bei joins with Liu Biao, but Lady Cai and Cai Mao successfully convince Liu Biao to keep his relative distant, and Liu Bei is stationed at Xinye. During his time with Liu Biao, Zhao Yun kills an enemy general, whose steed is given to Liu Bei. Liu Bei sends it to Liu Biao, who learns of the steed's bad luck towards its rider. Liu Bei, however, believes that life and death are pre-ordained and cares little for the horse's "curse".

Invited to a banquet, Liu Bei learns that Cai Mao wishes to kill him thanks to Sun Qian, and flees at the dusk of night. Cai Mao attempts to give chase, but Liu Bei is eventually able to escape.

Hoping to increase his army's strength, Liu Bei finds and employs Xu Shu as a strategist. Giving Xu command of his army, Liu Bei is able to defeat Cao Cao's men, who invade Xinye. Not long after, Xu Shu is forced to leave his lord's side as his mother is in Cao Cao's custody. Before leaving, Xu gives Liu Bei directions to Zhuge Liang's cottage, believing the recluse to be key to Liu's future. Following this advice, Liu Bei personally visits the cottage three times. On the third time, he is able to finally meet Zhuge Liang, and the two discuss Zhuge's Longzhong plan, which predicts a tripartite division of the land.

To convince Guan Yu and Zhang Fei of Zhuge's talents, Liu defers control of the Battle of Bowang Hill to Zhuge, who defeats Xiahou Dun and Yu Jin with an ambush and fire attack. After this, Liu Bei is visited by Liu Qi, who fears the wrath of Lady Cai as she wishes for her son to succeed Liu Biao. Liu Bei eventually advises his relative to lure Zhuge Liang into a library and to remove the ladder, forcing Zhuge to help Liu Qi.

After this, Liu Biao dies with Liu Cong being named as heir, and Cao Cao sends an even larger force to attack Liu Bei. Knowing that Liu Cong would not oppose Cao Cao, Liu Bei decides to flee further south to Sun Quan's domain. Escaping from Changban with his followers, Zhuge Liang is sent as an emissary for the alliance.

sections.heading: Red Cliffs and the Conflict for Jing Province
sections.content:
The Sun-Liu alliance leads to the halt of Cao Cao's southern invasion. With Liu Cong dead, and most of Cao Cao's forces scattered, Liu Bei begins attacking the southern cities of Jingzhou while Sun Quan is preoccupied with Cao Cao's remaining forces at Yiling and Nanjun. Defeating the four governors, Han Xuan, Jin Xuan, Zhao Fan, and Liu Du, Liu Bei successfully takes control of southern Jing, before taking the most of the central part from the battle-weary Sun forces.

Citing Liu Qi's status as Liu Biao's true successor, Liu Bei is temporarily left at peace until Liu Qi dies, and Zhou Yu presses the issue once more. Hoping to increase ties, a marriage request is sent to have Liu Bei go to Jiangdong to marry Lady Sun. Despite knowing a trap, Zhuge Liang sends his lord off with Zhao Yun, and a box containing special instructions for Zhao.

With instructions to make the wedding as public as possible, Liu Bei personally visited Qiao Xuan and Lady Wu, both of whom had no idea of the wedding. Later, when assassin hid to kill Liu Bei, he kowtowed instead to Lady Wu asking for execution instead. This led Lady Wu to call out the assassins herself, who were spared at Liu Bei's urgings. Liu Bei was thus able to marry Lady Sun without incident

To counter this, Zhou Yu and Sun Quan attempted to keep Liu Bei complacent instead, sending him to an endless spree of hunting trips and banquets. Zhao Yun, however, intervenes by lying of an attack by Cao Cao, pushing Liu Bei and Lady Sun to flee Jiangdong. Later on, Zhang Song arrives at Jing Province, with the offer of helping Liu Bei take over Yizhou from Liu Zhang. At Pang Tong's behest, the offer is accepted, and Liu Bei offers aid to Liu Zhang against Zhang Lu.

sections.heading: Building foundations
sections.content:
Liu Bei was given three options by Pang Tong, to either head straight for the heart of Cheng Du to depose Liu Zhang, defeat Gao Pei and Yang Huai and take Fu pass, or to remain in Jingzhou. Liu picked the second options. Tricking both men into coming into their tent and seeing through their assassination attempt, Liu Bei takes Fu pass with ease. Unfortunately, when they attempt to advance from there, Pang Tong is ambushed and killed by Zhang Ren, and Liu Zhang forms a truce with Zhang Lu to fight Liu Bei. Liu is forced to ask for Zhuge Liang's reinforcements.

Once Zhuge Liang, Zhang Fei and Zhao Yun arrive, Liu Bei decides to fight Zhang Lu first at Jiameng Gate. Making use of the corrupt Yang Song, Liu Bei not only defeats Zhang Lu but is also able to recruit the valiant Ma Chao. Ma Chao is later able to intimidate Liu Zhang into surrendering Chengdu, leaving the entire province under Liu Bei's command.

sections.heading: King of Hanzhong and Emperor
sections.content:
Shortly after, Cao Cao, however, defeats Zhang Lu and takes the Hanzhong region. To fight Cao Cao, Liu Bei gives the southern portion of Jingzhou to Sun Quan in exchange for a joint attack on Hefei and Hanzhong.

Defeating Cao Cao's force in the battle, Liu bei declares himself as King of Hanzhong, mirroring the title to his ancestor, Liu Bang. Liu Bei's victory would be short-lived; Guan Yu would be killed at Maicheng and Liu's territories in Jingzhou would then be seized by Sun Quan.

Grief-stricken, Liu Bei wishes to launch a punitive expedition but is calmed down by Zhuge Liang and other officials. Eventually, Cao Pi succeeds Cao Cao and replaces the Han Dynasty with Wei instead. As the only remaining member of the imperial family that's in a position to oppose Cao Pi, Liu Bei is declared emperor. Later on, Liu is visited by Zhang Fei, who finally convinces his oath brother to lead an attack on Wu. This would be the last time, Liu Bei would see Zhang, as he would be murdered by his own men, with his head being taken to Wu.

Enraged beyond belief, Liu Bei began leading his troops in Wu territory at an astonishing speed, defeating Sun Quan's commander in many skirmishes. His rage eventually leads Fu Shiren and Mi Fang, both of whom had betrayed Guan Yu during his siege at Fancheng, to return with Ma Zhong's head and an attempt to rejoin Liu Bei. Instead, Liu orders Guan Xing to execute them both and offer their heads to Guan Yu. Sun Quan, hoping to tie all loose ends, sends in Fan Jiang and Zhang Da, Zhang Fei's killers, to Liu Bei as well as the promise to return both Lady Sun and the lost territories of Jingzhou back to Liu. Having Fan and Zhang killed by Zhang Bao, Liu Bei flatly rejects the offer and swears to not stop until Sun Quan's head is sent to him on a platter.

Liu Bei soon ends up fighting against Lu Xun, a young but skilled strategist of the Wu army. Lu Xun's defensive strategies slow Shu progress and stalls out until the heat of summer. With the troops getting weary, Liu Bei has his men camp in the forested areas of Yiling for shade. This decision would prove to be a fatal mistake, as Lu Xun burned Liu Bei's camps, forcing him to flee. Joined by Guan Xing and Zhang Bao, Liu Bei was rescued at the last second thanks to Zhao Yun, sent by Zhuge Liang.

Making it back to Baidicheng, Liu Bei was wrought with guilt by acting on anger, refusing honest advice from trustworthy officials and generals, and for costing the lives of numerous vassals. With very little life left, Liu Bei called both Zhao Yun and Zhuge Liang forward. Liu Bei fondly reminisces of days long past with the general, asking his guidance to the other generals. Noticing the late Ma Liang's brother, Ma Su in the room, Liu Bei politely asks him to leave before speaking with Zhuge Liang.

Asking his chancellor of his thoughts on Ma, Zhuge Liang praises Ma's skill at a young age and believes him to be able to lead the state in the future. In response, Liu Bei openly voices his concern, believing Ma Su to be a braggart, and warns Zhuge to not place Ma Su in any important position. He then apologizes for failing to heed Zhuge Liang's advice of not attacking Wu and for failing to accomplish the aspirations his vassal had of him since the day they first met. His final command to Zhuge Liang is to support his son, or if his son proves to be incompetent, to take the throne for himself.

Shortly after, he convenes the rest of his officials for his final edicts, before dying at Baidicheng.

sections.heading: Trivia
sections.content:
- An inside joke with Japanese fans regarding his character design in the Dynasty Warriors series was his previously plain-looking appearance. Until his Dynasty Warriors 6 redesign, they nicknamed him "Citizen" (民, tami) or "Mob" (the common soldiers who create the bulk of armies) and jokingly remarked that the normal soldiers killed during cutscenes were Liu Bei's clones. The nickname became popular for Liu Bei since he often declares that he is fighting "for the people" (民のため, Tami no tame).

- In Liu Bei's artworks for Dynasty Warriors 4 and 5, he is somehow drawn wielding his 4th/Level 10 weapon as opposed to his default 3rd/Level 7-to-9 weapon.
Likewise in the Shu Kingdom's opening cutscene for Act 1 of Musou Mode in Dynasty Warriors 4, the sword rendered in Liu Bei's hands throughout happens to be Cao Cao's Level 7-to-9/3rd weapon as opposed to his own (likely an error).
